UI Design · Product Page

CASABREW

A product page for a premium espresso machine, designed to guide users through features, build desire, and convert interest into purchase.

Designer Ada
Role UI Designer
Tools Figma
Duration 2 months
Overview

An informational product experience built on design principles

CASABREW is a prototype product page for a premium home espresso machine, designed to inform, engage, and guide users through a purchase decision using 20 core UI design concepts.

The brief

I was tasked with creating an informational website or app prototype that communicates something new to users, such as a product or tutorial topic. The project required me to incorporate 20 required design concepts, researching existing websites for industry standards, and applying all instructor feedback throughout the process.

20

Design principles applied

Every screen of the CASABREW prototype was built to demonstrate a specific, researched UI principle in context.

4

Pages in the final prototype

Home, About, Features, and Where to Buy: each page designed to move the user one step closer to understanding the product.

2

Months of iteration

From proposal to final prototype, the design went through multiple rounds of feedback and refinement.

3

Key principles spotlighted

Jakob's Law, the Spatial Contiguity Principle, and the Zeigarnik Effect are highlighted as core decisions in the process.

Goals

Clear interface. Engaging visuals. All 20 principles.

The goal was to design and build a functional prototype that effectively communicates information to users through a clear interface, engaging visuals, and user-friendly navigation, while ensuring the inclusion of all 20 required principles of design.

Beyond the checklist, I aimed to create something that felt like a real product page: one that could exist in the world and genuinely convince a user the CASABREW espresso machine was worth buying.

I researched existing product pages (appliance brands, DTC coffee companies, premium electronics retailers) to inform layout conventions, call-to-action placement, and feature communication patterns.

Project Proposal

The design thinking, documented

Process

From proposal to prototype

A structured, research-informed process guided each phase of the project, from defining design principles to final delivery.

01

Project Proposal

I outlined the different ways I planned to apply the 20 design principles learned in the course. Each principle was mapped to a specific section or interaction of the CASABREW site, creating a blueprint for my design decisions. The proposal served as both a planning document and a design rationale.

02

Research & Benchmarking

I studied existing product pages and marketing sites to understand industry conventions: where navigation sits, how features are presented, what makes a CTA compelling, and how typography and imagery work together to build desire for a product. These patterns informed every layout decision I made.

03

UI Design in Figma

I built the full prototype in Figma across four pages: Home, About, Features, and Where to Buy. Each screen was designed to demonstrate specific principles in action, with annotations to document how and why each principle was applied.

04

Feedback & Iteration

I collected and applied instructor feedback throughout the project. Each round of revisions touched layout, hierarchy, spacing, and principle documentation, strengthening both the visual design and conceptual clarity.

05

Final Prototype & Delivery

I delivered the completed prototype as an interactive Figma file alongside this process documentation. The final design incorporates all 20 required principles and presents the CASABREW espresso machine as a coherent, desirable product.

Design Principles

Three principles in focus

Of the 20 UI design concepts applied throughout the project, three shaped the most significant design decisions.

🧭

Jakob's Law

Users prefer websites that work like most other websites. I applied this by keeping the navigation bar at the top, ensuring all links behaved as expected, and following established layout conventions so the site felt immediately familiar and trustworthy.

🖼

Spatial Contiguity Principle

Users understand information better when related text and visuals are placed together. The CASABREW Features page integrates annotations directly alongside product imagery, so users never have to hunt for the explanation of what they're looking at.

🔄

Zeigarnik Effect

People remember unfinished tasks more than completed ones. A carousel indicator functions as a visible progress bar through the product features, making users aware of how many remain and drawing them to complete the sequence even if they step away.

Final Project

The CASABREW product page

A complete four-page prototype I designed to help users understand the machine's features and see why it would be a valuable purchase.

☕
CASABREW
Click to view the interactive Figma prototype
View Prototype ↗

Home page

A hero section with the product front and center, a clear headline, and a primary CTA. Navigation is persistent and predictable, applying Jakob's Law from the first moment.

About page

Brand story and product origin, designed to build trust and emotional connection with the CASABREW machine before users evaluate its features.

Features page

An annotated feature carousel showcasing the machine's key capabilities. The Zeigarnik progress indicator keeps users engaged through every feature, and Spatial Contiguity ensures each annotation lands alongside its visual.

Where to buy

Retailer listings and purchase options, designed to remove friction at the decision point and give users a clear next step after being convinced by the product.

Reflection

What I learned from this project

This project was as much about learning to think like a designer as it was about producing a finished prototype.

01

Design is more than aesthetics

Applying 20 named principles made it clear to me that every layout decision has a reason behind it. Good design isn't decoration; it's a system of choices that guides user behavior.

02

Intuition needs frameworks

Before this project, my design decisions were often instinctive. Naming the principle behind each choice turned my intuition into an articulable skill I can explain, defend, and teach.

03

Accessibility is not optional

Designing with accessibility in mind throughout (contrast, hierarchy, label clarity) reinforced for me that accessibility is a core quality of good design, not a checklist item added at the end.

04

Feedback accelerates growth

Each round of instructor feedback surfaced blind spots I wouldn't have caught on my own. Learning to receive and apply critique quickly is one of the most transferable skills I developed.

05

Research grounds decisions

Benchmarking real product pages before designing gave me a foundation of industry conventions to build on. Knowing what users already expect made it easier to meet those expectations intentionally.

06

Process documentation matters

Building this process page alongside the prototype made my design thinking visible and shareable. Being able to communicate not just what I made but why I made it is what separates a portfolio piece from a school project.
